          material is approximately $3,113,561 during the 2025 fiscal year. This includes
          $202,708 in supplies and $2,910,853 in labor.
          The estimated cost of this report for the Department of the Navy (DON) is
          $98,398. The estimated total cost for supporting the DON budget justification
OPN 2
          material is approximately $3,113,561 during the 2025 fiscal year. This includes
          $202,708 in supplies and $2,910,853 in labor.
          The estimated cost of this report for the Department of the Navy (DON) is
          $22,577. The estimated total cost for supporting the DON budget justification
OPN 3
          material is approximately $3,113,561 during the 2025 fiscal year. This includes

          $37,623. The estimated total cost for supporting the DON budget justification
OPN 4

              UNCLASSIFIED

                                     Volume 5 - vi
                         Department of Defense Appropriations Act, 2026

Other Procurement, Navy

For procurement, production, and modernization of support equipment and materials not
otherwise provided for, Navy ordnance (except ordnance for new aircraft, new ships, and ships
authorized for conversion); the purchase of passenger motor vehicles for replacement only;
expansion of public and private plants, including the land necessary therefore, and such lands and
interests therein, may be acquired, and construction prosecuted thereon prior to approval of title;
and procurement and installation of equipment, appliances, and machine tools in public and
private plants; reserve plant and Government and contractor-owned equipment layaway,
$17,904,970,000 to remain available for obligation until September 30, 2028, of which $1,076,000
shall be available for the Navy Reserve and the Marine Corps Reserve: Provided, That such funds
are also available for the maintenance, repair, and modernization of ships.


Fiscal Year (FY) 2026 Overseas Operations Costs funding accounted for in the Base budget total
$5,050,000.

 Description:
 This budget line item funds procurement of passenger-carrying vehicles consisting of buses, automobiles, ambulances, and sport utility vehicles (SUVs), and carryall passenger vans up to 9200 lbs. Gross
 Vehicle Weight Rating (GVWR). These vehicles are utilized by Naval Operating Forces and Shore Activities for essential transportation of personnel in the execution of official Navy business.

 Buses procured range from 20 to 66 passenger school buses, shuttle buses, intercity buses, and ambulance buses, which provide the most cost-effective means to transport large groups of personnel between
 various locations. Buses are used to transport sailors/airmen and reserve personnel for flight/ship logistic related assignments, mandatory military training and exercises, and for transportation of personnel
 between administrative areas, ships/airfields, and industrial areas on a daily basis (both scheduled and intermittent).

 Automobiles are used to transport small groups of personnel, on and off base, for various work-related activities. Law enforcement automobiles provide essential transportation services to insure optimum
 responsiveness in support of DoD intelligence and base security missions. They are used in Naval intelligence, investigative and surveillance operations, security patrols, and other law enforcement activities.

 Ambulances are used by the Medical Corps at Navy hospitals, clinics, and by Naval Expeditionary Medical Command Units. Modular ambulances are used for emergency transport of personnel where
 emergency medical services are provided in route. Field ambulances provide the same emergency service, with the four-wheel drive capability to access off-road remote sites in support of field units. Patient
 transports are used for transporting stabilized patients to specialized care/other medical facilities. Ambulance conversion/MEDEVAC buses have the capability to transport a combination of ambulatory and/or
 stretcher-borne patients.

 Sport utility vehicles are utilized to transport both personnel and/or small loads of cargo, such as supplies, materials, and equipment. Carryall passenger vans are used for transporting sailors, flight crews,
 maintenance, and civilian personnel to work sites or for other mission related activities.

Note: Totals in this Exhibit P-40 set may not be exact or sum exactly due to rounding.

 Justification:
 Passenger-Carrying Vehicles - PW (BSVE/NTVE) - FY26 funding supports recapitalization of Navy-owned Passenger-Carrying Vehicle assets in locations not currently supported by General Services
 Administration and commercial leasing options are either not available or leasing costs would exceed 90% of the purchase price. These vehicles have surpassed their shore life expectancies, exceeded
 established maintenance thresholds, and repair parts are scarce.

 Naval Special Warfare (NSW) requires safety response vehicles at each type of high-risk training event. Training is conducted in areas that are inaccessible by ambulances and NSW has responsibly to get
 personnel requiring urgent medical care from a remote training site to a hard surface road for hand off to an ambulance. Naval Special Warfare Unit Level Training (ULT) and Squadron Integration Training (SIT)
 requires a fleet of Passenger-Carrying Vehicles to move personnel in small units, task groups and squadron size elements for participation in specialized training, exercises, redeployments certification training
 and events designed to improve tactics, techniques and procedures. NSW operates a large training pipeline with over 600 students involved in Basic Underwater Demolition School and Advanced Training
 Command curriculum. Personnel move between a number of offsite ranges and terrain models to work in unique operational environments that enhance training and ensure the Naval Special Warfare personnel
 train under environmental conditions expected in a combat zone.

 Description:
 General Purpose Trucks - PW (BSVE/NTVE) - This budget line item funds Naval Operating Forces and Shore Activities with various sizes of Cargo Pickup Trucks, Stake Trucks, Maintenance Utility Vans/Trucks,
 as well as Panel/Cargo Vans/Trucks of commercial design.

 General Purpose Trucks (GPT) transport Naval Special Warfare (NSW) SEALs and Special Warfare Combatant-craft Crewmen (SWCC); combat enablers and Combat Service Support (CSS) personnel; and
 equipment for small unit training. NSW training is conducted in a number of operating environments that include desert, mountain, and beach type terrains where GPT's are well suited to maneuver. GPTs
 provide an efficient way to transport 4 - 5 personnel, tow cargo trailers and carry training equipment and are heavily relied on as many training areas are outside of the immediate area of NSW's key home
 stations in San Diego, CA, Little Creek, VA and Stennis, MS. Vehicles are provided across dozens of operational commands, training commands and staff headquarters, and are vital to effectively transporting
 SEALs/SWCCs and their support elements.

 Shore Activities use Cargo Pickup Trucks to transport personnel and required equipment in support of fleet operations where such mobility is necessary to support the mission. The Maintenance Utility Vans/
 Trucks are used to transport tools/materials necessary for maintenance personnel performing facility maintenance. Panel Vans and Multi-Stop Trucks are used primarily for the movement of material/equipment
 requiring protection in an enclosed van-type body. Stake Trucks are used to move palletized material/equipment from warehouses to job sites/users at various locations.

Note: Totals in this Exhibit P-40 set may not be exact or sum exactly due to rounding.

 Justification:
 General Purpose Trucks - PW (BSVE/NTVE) - FY26 funding supports recapitalization of Navy-owned General Purpose Truck assets in locations not currently supported by General Services Administration
 (GSA) and commercial leasing options are either not available or leasing costs would exceed 90% of the purchase price. These vehicles have surpassed their shore life expectancies, exceeded established
 maintenance thresholds, and parts availability is becoming scarce.

 Funding provides for vehicles that directly support the Maritime Special Operations Training. This includes vehicles across twenty-one (21) NSW operational commands covering areas such as weapons ranges,
 military free fall parachuting, combat diving, unmanned air systems and special boat training as well as eight (8) staff headquarters and the NSW "A" and "C" schools inclusive of Basic Underwater Demolition
 (BUDS) training and advanced skills courses. In addition, general purpose trucks provide direct support to SEAL, Special Boat and Combat Support Teams conducting training for high-risk deployments. General
 Purpose Trucks are considered the primary class of vehicle to transport small arms weapons, dive gear, parachutes, special operations support gear and personnel in small units.

 Description:
 This funding directly enables the Naval Expeditionary Combat Forces (NECF) to execute vital missions across the spectrum of conflict, from supporting Distributed Maritime Operations and Expeditionary
 Advanced Base Operations to providing Humanitarian Assistance/Disaster Relief and Defense Support of Civil Authorities. It achieves this by:

 -Procuring and Recapitalizing Mission-Essential Equipment: This budget line provides for the procurement and recapitalization of construction, maintenance, and logistics equipment essential for:

 o Operational Plan (OPLAN) Execution: Equipping NECF to successfully execute their roles in a contested maritime environment, directly supporting Fleet operations and Maritime Logistics Campaign Plans.
 o Partner Nation Support: Building and strengthening alliances through joint training exercises and providing critical assistance during Humanitarian Assistance/Disaster Relief (HADR) operations.
 o Contested Logistics: Bridging the gap between shore-based logistics and the fleet through efficient reload, resupply, refuel, revive, and repair operations.

 - Addressing Critical Capability Gaps:

 o Establishing a Pacific-focused Naval Cargo Handling Battalion (NCHB): This enhances fleet lethality and operational availability by providing forward-deployed Vertical Launch System (VLS) reload capability
 for surface combatants.
 o Expanding Underwater Construction and Port Damage Repair Capacity: The addition of six Underwater Construction Dive Detachments and six Port Damage Repair Companies bolsters the NECF's ability to
 rapidly respond to contingencies and ensure operational access in contested environments.

 - Averting a Looming Readiness Crisis:

 o With 95% of NECF construction, weight handling, and material handling equipment projected to reach end-of-service life by 2030, this recapitalization effort is not just an investment in future capabilities, but a
 critical requirement to prevent a significant degradation in operational readiness.

 Types of equipment funded by this LI are:

 Earth Moving Equipment:


LI 6024 - Construction & Maint Equip                                                                    UNCLASSIFIED
                                                                                                                                                                                                        Volume 5 - 19
Navy                                                                                                           Page 1 of 26                                  P-1 Line #143
                                                                                               UNCLASSIFIED
Exhibit P-40, Budget Line Item Justification: PB 2026 Navy                                                                                                         Date: June 2025
Appropriation / Budget Activity / Budget Sub Activity:                           P-1 Line Item Number / Title:
1810N: Other Procurement, Navy / BA 05: Civil Engineering Support Equip / BSA 1: 6024 / Construction & Maint Equip
Civil Engineering Support Equipment
ID Code (A=Service Ready, B=Not Service Ready): A                       Program Elements for Code B Items: N/A                                   Other Related Program Elements: N/A
Line Item MDAP/MAIS Code: N/A
 Earth moving equipment is not just about moving dirt; it's about enabling the Naval Construction Force (NCF) to rapidly establish, operate from, and sustain operations in austere and contested environments.
 This equipment forms the very foundation upon which the NCF executes its critical mission set:
 - Advanced Base Construction: From clearing landing zones to building expeditionary airfields and port facilities, this equipment is essential for projecting power and sustaining operations in remote and
 challenging locations.
 - Airfield Damage Repair: In a contested environment, rapid airfield repair is crucial to maintaining air superiority. This equipment enables the NCF to quickly repair runways and taxiways, ensuring uninterrupted
 air operations.
 - Port Damage Repair: Maintaining open and functioning seaports is critical for logistics and force projection. This equipment empowers the NCF to rapidly repair damaged piers, dredging channels, and restoring
 port operations, ensuring the uninterrupted flow of supplies and personnel.

 Miscellaneous Construction Equipment:

 The Navy Expeditionary Combat Forces (NECF) utilize a diverse range of miscellaneous construction equipment categorized into four primary types:
 1. Concrete and Asphalt Equipment: This category encompasses equipment used in concrete and asphalt production and placement, including portable concrete mixers, rock crushers, asphalt and water
 distributors, aggregate spreaders, and asphalt/rubberized compound heating kettles. This equipment is crucial for constructing and maintaining advanced bases, forward port facilities, runways, taxi aprons, and
 other paved work areas.
 2. Pneumatic & Drilling Equipment: This category includes portable air compressors of varying sizes and capacities, rock drills for quarry operations, pile hammers and extractors for constructing and maintaining
 waterfront structures (causeways, docks, piers, and wharves), earth augers for utility installation (electrical and communications), and well drilling machines for providing potable water at campsites and forward
 operating bases.
 3. Power Generation & Lighting: This category comprises portable floodlight trailers equipped with 6kW generators, enabling 24/7 construction operations. Additionally, standardized generators, managed under
 the Department of Defense Mobile Electric Power Program (PM-MEP), provide power for tools, runway lighting, and backup electrical systems.
 4. Maintenance & Support Equipment: This category includes welders, decontamination apparatus, mobile machine shops, and shredders. This equipment supports various maintenance, repair, and construction
 activities, as well as personnel and equipment decontamination.

 Cranes (Weight Handling Equipment):
 The Navy Expeditionary Combat Forces (NECF) employ a variety of cranes for weight handling, including truck-mounted, wheel-mounted, and crawler cranes. Truck-mounted cranes, featuring either lattice or
 hydraulic booms, range in capacity from 25 to 300 tons. Wheel-mounted cranes, utilizing hydraulic booms, offer capacities from 8 to 200 tons. Crawler cranes are specifically designed for dragline and clamshell
 operations in challenging terrain inaccessible to truck or wheel-mounted cranes. These are particularly valuable for Navy Expeditionary Logistic Forces in performance of Vertical Launch Operations, and Naval
 Construction Forces for Expeditionary Port Damage Repair Ops.

 Expeditionary Material Handling Equipment (ExMHE):
 This program invests in critical Expeditionary Material Handling Equipment (ExMHE) to meet operational demands and replace obsolete, non-repairable equipment used across global Navy operations. ExMHE is
 essential for equipping and sustaining expeditionary forces, empowering them to effectively execute combat, combat support, and combat service support missions worldwide.

 In addition to supporting Expeditionary Combat Forces, the funds also enable PW mission by funding recapitalization of construction and maintenance equipment essential to shore installation operations. Public
 Works personnel use this equipment to sustain the infrastructure that underpins warfighter readiness, installation resilience, and operational continuity across the Navy and Marine Corps enterprise.

 This capability supports a wide range of critical functions from airfield and road maintenance to utility system excavation, debris clearance, and emergency response. Though the equipment parallels that used by
 Expeditionary units, it is configured and employed specifically for long-term infrastructure support at fixed shore installations, where aging assets and increasing maintenance costs challenge mission execution.
 Timely investment in this equipment ensures PW remains capable of delivering responsive, safe, and efficient infrastructure support across the globe, whether enabling force deployment, recovery from natural
 disasters, or execution of long-range sustainment priorities. Funding PW capability under this line reinforces the Navy's ability to sustain shore-based infrastructure in direct support of force readiness and long-
 term mission success.


LI 6024 - Construction & Maint Equip                                                           UNCLASSIFIED

Civil Engineering Support Equipment
ID Code (A=Service Ready, B=Not Service Ready): A                      Program Elements for Code B Items: N/A                                  Other Related Program Elements: N/A
Line Item MDAP/MAIS Code: N/A
 [P40A / Cranes - Weight Handling Equipment (WHE) - Expeditionary]: includes Truck or Wheel-Mounted Cranes, Straddle Lifts, and Crawler Cranes. Truck Mounted Cranes have either lattice or hydraulic booms
 and range in size from 25 to 300 tons. Wheel- mounted cranes have hydraulic booms and range in size from 8 to 200 tons.

 [P40A / Earthmoving Equipment - Expeditionary]: Earth Moving Equipment for Expeditionary Navy is funding end items to support the Pier Damage Repair and Airfield Damage Repair Capabilities of the
 Seabees. In addition, recapitalization of over age equipment for the Naval Beach Groups, Naval Special Warfare and Navy Medical communities is also funded. The recapitalization of equipment in these units
 supports ship to shore and beach operations, expeditionary medical facility set up, and special operations.

 [P40A / MOBILE ELEVATING WORK PLATFORMS (MEWP) - PW]: Mobile Elevating Work Platforms (MEWP) - also known as Aerial Work Platforms (AWP) or manlifts. These can be of various sizes and
 lengths ranging anywhere from 40 feet to 120 feet and up to provide access to hard-to-reach areas on construction sites and on the piers.

 [P40A / MISC ELECTRICAL CONSTRUCTION EQUIPMENT - PW]: Miscellaneous Electrical Construction Equipment - This group consists of mobile Air Compressors and Arc Welders of various sizes and
 capacities for construction and maintenance projects; portable Floodlight Trailer/Generator Sets to provide around-the-clock lighting/power to power tools, runway lighting, and backup systems for electrical power
 distribution. This equipment is part of the DOD Mobile Electric Power Program (PM-MEP) which provides reliable standardized generators for all DOD components.

 [P40A / Miscellaneous Construction Equipment - Expeditionary]: Miscellaneous Construction Equipment - consists of Asphalt Pavers, Concrete Mixers, Water/Asphalt Distribution Trucks, decontamination
 apparatus, machine shop trailers and shredders, Snow Plow Rollover Trucks. This equipment is used for a variety of maintenance, repair and construction operations and for purification and decontamination of
 personnel and equipment.

 [P40A / Acquisition Support - Expeditionary]: Acquisition support provides for government oversight of the acquisition process, program management support, acquisition engineering and technical support,
 review and evaluation
 of design data, writing of contracts and Contract Data Requirements Lists (DCRL), contract deliverable monitoring, evaluation of Engineering Change Proposals (ECP), configuration control and quality
 assurance, status reports and technical briefings, collection of performance metrics, and all other support efforts directly related to the delivery of equipment.

 [P40A / Acquisition Support]: Acquisition support provides for government execution and oversight of the acquisition process, program management support, acquisition engineering and technical support, review
 and evaluation of design data, writing of contracts and Contract Data Requirements Lists (DCRL), contract deliverable monitoring, evaluation of Engineering Change Proposals (ECP), configuration control and
 quality assurance, status reports and technical briefings, collection of performance metrics, and all other support efforts directly related to the procurement and delivery of equipment.

 Justification:
 Expeditionary:
 The FY26 funding increase supports the Navy Expeditionary Forces by continuing the initial outfitting of Airfield Damage Repair equipment to achieve Initial Operating Capability (IOC). It also funds Pier
 Damage Repair (PDR) equipment sets necessary to meet IOC requirements. Additionally, this funding will provide essential equipment for the initial outfitting of the new NCHB-3, enabling it to fulfill its Required
 Operational Capabilities (ROC) in a Projected Operational Environment (POE), which includes rearming, refueling, and resupplying fleet assets.

 The increased investment in Construction and Maintenance Equipment for Expeditionary Forces includes $4.380 million for Cranes and Weight Handling Equipment (WHE), $4.016 million for Miscellaneous
 Construction Equipment, and $23.131 million for Material Handling Equipment. This investment addresses critical shortages in items listed on the units' Table of Allowance (TOA) and facilitates the necessary
 recapitalization of over-aged equipment.

 The emphasis on recapitalizing older equipment is vital for improving readiness. Without reliable equipment in the fleet, there is significant risk in meeting in-theater operational mission requirements and
 deadlines for building, repairing, and maintaining critical infrastructure, such as roads, runways, and undertaking demolition, excavation, and underground utility work.

 Public Works:
 EARTHMOVING EQUIPMENT - PW - $1.902M increase supports the strategic recapitalization of NAVFAC'S aging and degraded Shore fleet, which is essential to maintaining resilient mission-ready installations.
 Much of the existing fleet is aged and has outlived its expected lifecycle, leading to increasing reliability concerns and escalating maintenance costs. This degradation impacts our ability to execute critical
 infrastructure projects on schedule and sustain operational readiness across the shore installations.

 This funding enables the replacement of mission-critical equipment required for key PW functions, including airfield and roadway construction and repair, utility excavation, facility demolition and emergency
 response. These capabilities directly affect the warfighter readiness. Without dependable equipment, project delays and mission degradation become likely, posing risks to force deployment timelines and the
 sustainment of critical infrastructure. Recapitalization this fleet ensures PW remains agile, responsive and fully equipped to delivery safe, reliable, and timely support to naval shore installations and warfighter
 readiness.

Appropriation / Budget Activity / Budget Sub Activity:                           P-1 Line Item Number / Title:
1810N: Other Procurement, Navy / BA 05: Civil Engineering Support Equip / BSA 1: 6027 / Fire Fighting Equipment

Initial Spares ($ in Millions)                               -              -               -              -              -               -              -               -         -            -            -              -
Flyaway Unit Cost ($ in Dollars)                             -              -               -              -              -               -              -               -         -            -            -              -
Gross/Weapon System Unit Cost ($ in Dollars)                 -              -               -              -              -               -              -               -         -            -            -              -


 Description:
 This budget line item funds procurement of essential emergency response vehicles for Installations, i.e., aircraft fire/rescue trucks, specialized shore-based ship response apparatus, and structural/brush fire
 trucks and hazardous emergency response trucks.

 The aircraft fire/rescue trucks are used at Naval Air Stations for combating aircraft fires and rescue of aircraft crews. The trucks range in size from a small 19,000-pound Gross Vehicle Weight Rating (GVWR)
 pickup with utility body and twin-engine firefighting unit to the 68,000 pound GVWR crash truck which carries 3,000 gallons of water and 410 gallons of foam. The structural/brush fire trucks and specialized shore-
 based shipboard emergency vehicles with up to 82,000-pound GVWR are used at Naval activities in the same manner as municipal fire trucks in fighting structural, ships, and grass/wildland fires.

 The Navy's investment in ships, aircraft, facilities, personnel, and equipment mandates having adequate fire protection in highest state of readiness to respond. The requested funds are needed to comply with
 current National Fire Protection Association (NFPA) standards, sustain the Navy Firefighting Fleet and maintain an effective Firefighting Fleet Modernization Program consistent with findings identified in the
 DoD IG Report: D-2003-121 DoD Fire and Emergency Services Program. The Firefighting Fleet requires increased annual investments to ensure emergency response vehicles are within specified service life
 for safety, technological improvements, and maintenance sustainability. Highly reliable trucks are required as the emergency vehicles are key to success. Some older crash response trucks do not meet roll
 safety criteria and require continued replacement to ensure safe working limits. As firefighter staffing is expanded for new mission requirements the all-hazards emergency response mission expands, the Navy's
 firefighting apparatus must evolve to provide additional capabilities for emergency medical services, rescue, confined space, hazardous materials, shipboard firefighting, etc.

 The ability to save lives and protect property is essential in supporting the Navy's mission. The role of these trucks is to provide the Navy's response to structural fire emergencies, ship fire incidents, aircraft
 rescue firefighting crashes, emergency medical services incidents, all hazards public safety, and emergency response to terrorism incidents, and weapons of mass destruction.

Note: Totals in this Exhibit P-40 set may not be exact or sum exactly due to rounding.

 Justification:
 FY2026 increased by ($1.753M) to procure new shore-based ship emergency equipment (larger pump capacity pumpers and larger rescue vehicles).

 The standard recapitalization of equipment and increased replacement equipment costs to support the shore-based response to shipboard fires accounts for the baseline budget.

 Fire apparatus are essential tools for Navy firefighters to protect Navy assets (people, structures, aircraft, and ships) from uncontrolled fire when it occurs. New fire trucks (estimated 60) are required each year, to
 maintain minimal response capability and to safely respond to emergencies at our Navy Installation and in support of the fleet (aircraft and ships).

 Description:
 This budget line procures essential light and medium tactical vehicles to enhance the Navy Expeditionary Force's operational capabilities across a range of critical missions. These vehicles directly support
 Operational Plans, Partner Nations, and key Naval and Joint Force strategic initiatives, including Distributed Maritime Operations, Expeditionary Advanced Base Operations, and Pacific Posture initiatives. This
 investment bolsters the Navy's ability to operate in contested environments and provide Humanitarian Assistance & Disaster Relief (HADR) to allies and partners.
 Specifically, this procurement funds the following vehicles and their associated mission capabilities:
 - Medium Service Support Vehicle (MSSV): Enables Explosive Ordnance Disposal (EOD) forces to transport personnel and equipment necessary to execute Expeditionary Mine Counter Measures (ExMCM)
 missions.
 - Light Service Support Vehicle (LSSV): Provides the Navy Expeditionary Combat Force (NECF) with the mobility / hauling capacity required for transporting gear and equipment required to support Expeditionary
 Vertical Launch System (VLS) reloads, airfield and port damage repair, and maritime security operations.
 - Medium Tactical Vehicle Replacement (MTVR): Supports the movement of ordnance and heavy equipment for VLS reloads, air asset resupply and rearmament, and Maritime Prepositioning Force (MPF) on/
 offload operations.
 - Ultra-Light Tactical Vehicle (ULTV): Equips Naval Special Warfare (NSW) units with the agility needed for forward operations supporting Marine Corps Forces and Expeditionary Advanced Base Operations.
 - Joint Light Tactical Vehicle (JLTV) & High Mobility Multipurpose Wheeled Vehicle (HMMWV): Provide critical force protection and personnel transport capabilities for the Navy Expeditionary Force, enhancing
 security during convoy operations and in high-threat environments.
 This investment in tactical vehicles is crucial for maintaining the Navy Expeditionary Force's readiness and ability to project power and provide critical support across the full spectrum of naval operations.

 The procurement of HMMWVs in this budget line also supports the guarding and protection of the TRIDENT II (D5LE) missile while the missile stored, handled, or in a movement to and from the waterfront. These
 armored HMMWVs support security forces to deter, delay, and defeat unauthorized shore access to nuclear weapons at Strategic Weapons Facilities. Vehicles are procured to replace existing vehicles used in
 security patrols and missile movement convoys; replacement is based on meeting or exceeded service life due to age and usage.

 [P40A / Expeditionary HMMWV M1165A1 W VIK]: Funds support HMMWV recapitalization required to support Naval Beach Group unit readiness.

 [P40A / Expeditionary Light Tactical All-Terrain Vehicle]: Funding provided for Light Tactical All-Terrain Vehicles. These vehicles provide Expeditionary teams with a highly maneuverable and mobile form of
 transportation that is also low profile and easily transportable. The procurement of this platform for the Navy Special Warfare units provides commonality with Marine Corps enhancing interoperability across
 services.

 [P40A / Expeditionary MTVR - SLEP]: Funds provided for Medium Tactical Vehicle Replacement (MTVRs) Service Life Extension Program (SLEP). MTVR service life expectancy has increased from 21 to 42
 years making it necessary to perform midlife overhauls and SLEPs actions to extend performance for the life of the vehicles.

 [P40A / Expeditionary-Acquisition Support]: Acquisition support provides for government execution and oversight of the acquisition process, program management support, acquisition engineering and
 technical support, review and evaluation of design data, writing of contracts and Contract Data Requirements Lists (CDRL), contract deliverable monitoring, evaluation of Engineering Change Proposals (ECP),
 configuration control and quality assurance, status reports and technical briefings, collection of performance metrics, and all other support efforts directly related to the procurement and delivery of equipment.

Note: Totals in this Exhibit P-40 set may not be exact or sum exactly due to rounding.

 Justification:
 FY26 funding supports:
 - Medium Service Support Vehicle (MSSV) procurement and Medium Tactical Vehicle Replacement (MTVR) service life extension to outfit the Expeditionary Mine Countermeasures (ExMCM) companies
 with prime movers suitable to transport the 11M Rubber Hull Inflatable Boat (RHIB) in combat operations. Transportation of the 11M RHIB is necessary for the deployment of Unmanned Underwater Vehicles
 (UUVs), remotely-operated vehicles (ROVs), and Explosive Ordnance Disposal (EOD) divers to locate, identify, and neutralize mines to ensure the safe passage of critical fleet surface and submarine assets.
 Procurement in FY26 is necessary to ensure readiness of the ExMCM companies to conduct the ExMCM mission in non-permissive environments to support Operational Plans.
 - Light Service Support Vehicle (LSSV) procurement to outfit the newly activated Navy Cargo Handling Battalion to meet Vertical Launch System (VLS) reload Operational Plan capacity requirements and the
 Expeditionary Medical Facility (EMF) Table of Allowance (TOA) for forward deployed and rotational units across all Combatant Commands.
 - Joint Light Tactical Vehicle (JLTV) procurement to support combat mission requirements for the readiness and battlefield effectiveness of Navy Special Warfare (NSW) units. The JLTV is essential in meeting
 force protection requirements in high threat environments.
 - High Mobility Multipurpose Wheeled Vehicle (HMMWV) procurement to support troop movement in operational environments and nuclear weapons security.
 - Command, Control, Communications, Computers Intelligence, Surveillance, and Reconnaissance (C4ISR), Common Remotely Operated Weapons (CROW), and crew-served weapon vehicle integration kit
 procurement for multiple tactical vehicle platforms, ensuring the force has a common C4ISR solution and common weapon system integration solutions.

Initial Spares ($ in Millions)                               -           0.222           0.428             -              -               -              -               -         -            -    -            -
Flyaway Unit Cost ($ in Dollars)                             -              -               -              -              -               -              -               -         -            -    -            -
Gross/Weapon System Unit Cost ($ in Dollars)                 -              -               -              -              -               -              -               -         -            -    -            -


 Description:
 This budget line item funds the procurement of equipment to support Navy-Marine Corps amphibious operations, Logistics Over the Shore, and Joint Logistics Over the Shore operations. This provides the ability
 to execute the ship to shore transfer of vehicles, dry cargo, and liquid cargo, delivered over bare beaches, as well as through improved and semi-improved port facilities.

 [P5 / Joint Petroleum Over the Shore (JPOTS) Family of Systems (FoS)]: The Joint Petroleum Over The Shore (JPOTS) Family of Systems (FoS) will enable delivery of fuel to the high-water mark in support of
 Joint operations inside the weapons engagement zone.

 [P5 / Joint Petroleum Over The Shore Capability Set]: Funds provided for Joint Petroleum Over the Shore (JPOTS) capability set in support of Distributed Maritime Operations (DMO), Expeditionary Advanced
 Base Operations (EABO) and Maritime Prepositioning Force (MPF) operations as well as other ground forces ashore.

 [P5 / Unmanned Aerial System (UAS)]: Funds initial procurement and phased sustainment of UAS assets, additional Littoral Imaging System (LIS) Unmanned Aerial System (UAS) assets and their associated
 sustainment support. Enables on-demand littoral reconnaissance data without putting Sailors/vessels in the water to determine safe sea lane passage during operations.

Note: Totals in this Exhibit P-40 set may not be exact or sum exactly due to rounding.

 Justification:
 Funding increase of $31.619M from FY25 to FY26 supports:

 $1.383M in support of initial procurement of Littoral Imaging System (LIS) Unmanned Aerial System (UAS) to facilitate ship-to-shore entry operations through mapping and calculation of Modified Surf Index
 (MSI). Using imagery from the LIS UAS, sailors can map coastal terrain, water depth, and wave/current hazards (calculate MSI) prior to littoral entry. LIS rapidly generates shareable, geo-referenced maps of
 bathymetry, topography, and hydrodynamic hazards of areas of interest which directly supports Distributed Maritime Operations (DMO) CONOPs.

 $30.236M supports Joint Petroleum Over the Shore (JPOTS) equipment procurement to enhance the support of Marine Corps amphibious and Logistics Over the Shore (LOTS) operations through ship-to-shore
 transfer of bulk petroleum. This program is a key part of the Strategic Sealift Program. The Joint Petroleum Over the Shore (JPOTS) Family of Systems (FoS) will enable delivery of fuel to the high-water mark
 in support of Joint operations inside the weapons engagement zone while allowing Navy large-scale critical cargo assets to remain outside the range of enemy fires. JPOTS FoS meets a range of requirements
 and deployment options for the transfer of petroleum from sea-based sources to the shore at the high-water mark. The systems comprising the JPOTS Program include: Sea-based Petroleum Distribution System
 (SPDS), Distributed Littoral operations Fuel Transfer System (DLOFTS), Amphibious Bulk Liquid Transfer System (ABLTS), Towed Fuel Bladder with Reach From The Beach (TFB with RFTB) and the Low
 Profile Distribution System (LPDS).

Flyaway Unit Cost ($ in Dollars)                             -              -               -              -              -               -              -               -         -            -       -              -
Gross/Weapon System Unit Cost ($ in Dollars)                 -              -               -              -              -               -              -               -         -            -       -              -


 Description:
 The Navy Ashore Pollution Control Equipment program funds hazardous substance spill containment and recovery systems.

 Funding requirements for the Navy's oil spill program include procurement of oil spill containment booms and related deployment equipment. After initial containment with boom, shore activities can efficiently
 collect spilled oil with vacuum trucks. This equipment will enable the Navy to meet the requirements established by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) in the National Contingency Plan which
 requires rapid and effective response to oil spills. The revised National Spill Contingency Plan mandates that the DoD and the Navy assume responsibility for their own oil and hazardous substance spills. These
 broad responsibilities require the Navy to maintain sufficient spill response equipment for the Navy activities worldwide, such as oil spill containment systems and recovery systems. The severe oil spills in Alaska,
 California and the Gulf of Mexico have increased the public's sensitivity to releases of oil into the environment.

 Justification:
 Additional procurement of shore-based oil spill response equipment in FY26 is required in order to improve mission readiness at Naval Installations. Without additional procurement, there will be increased
 maintenance costs to extend equipment service life and readiness which is impacted during increased maintenance cycle times.

 Description:
 SPECIAL PURPOSE VEHICLES/EQUIPMENT (BSO 52)
 This budget line includes procurement of specialized equipment used for repair, rearming, and resupplying operations at both Shore Installations and Advanced Naval Bases that support execution of home
 station training, Operational Plan (OPLAN) response, and support to Partner Nations. This equipment which is used by the Naval Construction Force, Naval Beach Group, and Naval Special Warfare directly
 support key Naval operating concepts and plans such as Distributed Maritime Operations (DMO), Expeditionary Advanced Base Operations (EABO), Maritime Logistics Campaign Plans, and INDOPACOM's
 Posture Initiatives. This equipment provides the Navy Expeditionary Combat Forces mission of supporting contested logistics and connecting shore logistics activities such as reload, resupply, refuel, revive,
 and repair operations to the afloat Naval force. This equipment additionally supports the execution of Humanitarian Assistance & Disaster Relief (HADR) support to Partner Nations and Defense Support of Civil
 Authorities (DSCA). Procurement of this equipment supports the establishment of a new Pacific focused Naval Cargo Handling Battalion (NCHB). A capability that provides Vertical Launched System (VLS)
 reload of surface combatants from multiple locations. Additionally, this procurement supports the addition of six Underwater Construction Dive Detachments and six Port Damage Repair Companies. Both of
 which are critical components of executing Port Damage Repair in a contested environment or in support of Partner Nations.

 This equipment empowers the Naval Construction Force, Naval Beach Group, and Naval Special Warfare to execute contested logistics operations, seamlessly connecting shore-based activities (reload,
 resupply, refuel, revive, and repair) with the afloat naval force. It is also instrumental in providing Humanitarian Assistance & Disaster Relief (HADR) and Defense Support of Civil Authorities (DSCA). Specifically,
 this procurement supports:
 -Establishment of a Pacific-focused Naval Cargo Handling Battalion (NCHB): Provides enhanced Vertical Launch System (VLS) reload capability for surface combatants from multiple locations.
 -Addition of six Underwater Construction Dive Detachments and six Port Damage Repair Companies: Bolsters the Navy's capacity for rapid port damage repair in contested environments and in support of
 Partner Nations.

 COMBAT CONSTRUCTION SUPPORT EQUIPMENT
 The equipment included in this program is used to provide responsive military construction support to forces during military operations, construction of base facilities, and in the conduct of limited defensive
 operations. These facilities and equipment are vital for maintaining the integrity and sustainability during contingency and wartime operations. Equipment items include containers required for prepacking and
 securing on-site storage of equipment to expedite mobilization, fuel storage tanks required for on-site storage of fuel, water purification units required for camp water treatment systems, water storage tanks
 (collapsible fabric) required for water treatment, storage and distribution systems, power distribution panel boards required for camp electrical distribution systems, and tension fabric structures required for
 expeditionary facilities to provide equipment maintenance and company shops, berthing, laundry, and combat operations centers.

Exhibit P-40, Budget Line Item Justification: PB 2026 Navy                                                                                                                Date: June 2025
Appropriation / Budget Activity / Budget Sub Activity:                           P-1 Line Item Number / Title:
1810N: Other Procurement, Navy / BA 05: Civil Engineering Support Equip / BSA 1: 6060 / Items less than $5 Million
Civil Engineering Support Equipment
ID Code (A=Service Ready, B=Not Service Ready): A                          Program Elements for Code B Items: N/A                                      Other Related Program Elements: N/A
Line Item MDAP/MAIS Code: N/A
 This equipment provides essential military construction support for a variety of operations, from base facility construction to limited defensive operations. These assets are vital for maintaining operational integrity
 and sustainability during contingency and wartime operations. Key procurements include:
 -Containers: For pre-packing and secure on-site equipment storage, enabling rapid mobilization.
 -Fuel Storage Tanks: For on-site fuel storage.
 -Water Purification Units & Collapsible Fabric Water Storage Tanks: For establishing camp water treatment, storage, and distribution systems.
 -Power Distribution Panel Boards: For camp electrical distribution systems.
 -Tension Fabric Structures: For establishing expeditionary facilities such as equipment maintenance and company shops, berthing areas, laundry facilities, and combat operations centers.

 This investment in specialized equipment and combat construction support systems is essential for enhancing the Navy's expeditionary readiness, operational reach, and ability to support a wide range of
 missions in diverse and challenging environments


 MOBILE UTILITIES SUPPORT EQUIPMENT (MUSE)
 Equipment consists of electric power generation plants, electric substations, and electric switch gear. MUSE provides short-term support for utility requirements resulting from equipment failures, changes in
 planning and programming, temporary replacement of utilities equipment which is out of service, ship support and testing, expeditionary military operations, and utilities outages resulting from natural disaster.
 Operations supported: submarine testing, ships' repair, retrofit and nuclear refueling, cold iron applications, serious utility system deficiencies, MILCON delay, and advanced base requirements.


 OCEAN CONSTRUCTION EQUIPMENT
 Specialized equipment and facilities components used to perform site selection, construction, inspection, maintenance, repair and removal fixed underwater and ocean facilities, and in support of shore-based
 hyperbaric. Utilization of funds from this program sustains capability to meet requirements for ocean facility site survey, construction, inspection, repair, and removal, resulting in the ability of the fleet to retain its
 readiness through utilization of its underwater facilities.

 ------------------

 SPECIAL PURPOSE VEHICLES (BSO 41) - This program provides various non-tactical special purpose vehicles, equipment, and trailers of commercial design to support mission critical and mission essential
 support to our Navy shore commands. Vehicles included in this category but not limited to aircraft refuelers, ammunition handling trucks, overhead maintenance & utility bucket trucks, pole and line trucks,
 runway & street sweepers, tank trucks, field servicing vehicles, waste collection/cleaner trucks, van/stake bed semi-trailers, low bed semi-trailers, tank trailers, wreckers, low speed electric vehicles, etc.

 ------------------

 SPECIAL PURPOSE VEHICLES (BSO 30) - This program procures Ballistic Engineered Armored Response Attack Trucks (BEARCAT) in the Navy Nuclear Deterrence Mission. These armored vehicles support
 security forces to deter, delay, and defeat unauthorized shore access to nuclear weapons at Strategic Weapons Facilities. Vehicles are procured to replace existing vehicles used in security patrols and missile
 movement convoys; replacement is based on meeting or exceeded service life due to age and usage.

 Maintenance Support Equipment (BSO 60): NECC N46 Equipment Procurement Funding Program for the procurement of specialized equipment used by NECC forces, but unsupported by a designated program
 office. This funding will provide NECC with procurement funds to support NECC facility modifications/infrastructures not covered under OMN or MILCON funding.

Note: Totals in this Exhibit P-40 set may not be exact or sum exactly due to rounding.

 Justification:
 Special Purpose Vehicles - Expeditionary/PW (BSO 52): FY26 increase ($1.804M-PW) due to increase support for fielding a fleet of Special Purpose Vehicles for Naval Special Warfare with expeditionary tent
 camp equipment used to support troops operating and training in the field; prime movers' trucks to tow special operations craft; and large trucks for moving ammunition, weapons and cargo. This logistic support
 is critical to train SEAL and Special Boat Teams for deployment. In addition, the increase of ($26.082) supports Expeditionary priority mission capabilities Mobile Boat Hoist (MBH) higher priority actions and new
 phased approach for NCHB-3 E/S and addresses table of allowance procurement wholeness for IOC units of action due to high priority items for Navy Response Plan (NRP).

 Combat Construction Support Equipment - Expeditionary: FY26 funding continues to support procurement of trailers, expeditionary facilities and fuel/water services. Funds support acquisition of expeditionary
 basing engineered capabilities to integrate joint standardization board (JSB) approved equipment to ensure interoperability, energy enhancements and commonality across the Navy and DoD. These materiel
 assets support core mission requirements and are essential to the readiness and battlefield effectiveness. The reduction in these funds (-20.055M) is being recapitalized to higher priority items in the Navy
 Response Plan.

 Mobile Utilities Support Equipment (MUSE): FY26 funds will be used to recapitalize the MUSE generator and substation equipment. Current inventory and condition assessment of MUSE power generation
 assets rated 77% of generation capability. The assessment also rated a zero percent capability to support CONUS prime power missions. The major risk is long term limited availability of MUSE (total and EPA
 compliant) generation assets with continued increases in unsupported MUSE missions.

 Ocean Construction Equipment: FY26 funds provided for procurement of specialized ocean construction equipment and will be utilized to procure underwater survey systems, underwater sonar systems, and
 underwater construction equipment.

 Special Purpose Vehicles (BSO 41): FY26 funds provided for procurement of various non-tactical special purpose vehicles, equipment, and trailers.

 Special Purpose Vehicles (BSO 30): FY26 funds provided for the procurement of BEARCATs to support mission requirements and replace existing vehicles used in security patrols and missile movement
 convoys; replacement is based on meeting or exceeded service life due to age and usage.

 Maintenance Support Equipment (BSO 60): FY26 funds support the procurement of a Hyperbaric Chamber in support of the EOD command at Naval Station Rota and 12K EE side-loader forklifts.

 Description:
 This budget line item funds procurement of physical security vehicles to include armored sedans and armored cargo/utility trucks assigned to Antiterrorism (AT), Counterintelligence (CI), and Counternarcotics
 (CN) missions in high threat Outside Continental United States (OCONUS) locations.

 Sedans and cargo/utility trucks are armored to various levels of protection and are on platforms of varying sizes and gross vehicle weights, dependent upon the level of threat and the operating environment.
 These vehicles are generically referred to as either Commercial Heavy Armored Vehicles (CHAVs) or Commercial Light Armored Vehicles (CLAVs).

Gross/Weapon System Unit Cost ($ in Dollars)                 -              -               -              -              -               -              -               -         -            -         -               -


 Description:
 Material Handling Equipment (MHE) and Shipboard Mobile Support Equipment (SMSE) is critical as ships cannot be mission-ready without the proper MHE/SMSE to move essential ammo, stores and material.
 Appropriate MHE is required to ensure efficient storage and handling operations within the U.S. Navy's various facilities and ships. The program focuses on shipboard and shore-based MHE/SMSE life-cycle
 management and in-service support for the procurement, overhaul, upgrade, and refurbishment. MHE/SMSE includes all self-propelled equipment approved for use in storage and handling operations in and
 around warehouses, shipyards, industrial plants, airfields, magazines, depots, terminals, aboard U.S. Navy ships, and at expeditionary bases.

 NAVY CASH PROGRAM: This program funds the procurement of the Navy Cash system. Navy Cash was developed in partnership with the US Department of Treasury to improve business processes, create
 workload efficiencies, and enhance the quality of life for Sailors and Marines on board ships. It enhances the efficiencies and security of automated pay delivery and safekeeping. Navy Cash automates all retail
 collections on ship and is a Treasury Direct Disbursing (TDD) compliant system in accordance with critical regulatory and Financial Improvement and Audit Readiness (FIAR) compliance standards mandated
 by DoD and Department of Treasury for achieving audit readiness of financial statements. Navy Cash system performs daily reporting of financial accountability as part of the TDD mandate Department of the
 Navy Office of The Assistant Secretary (Financial Management and Comptroller) dated 14 Nov 2018 and corrects an OUSD identified material weakness. Deployments are ongoing and are dependent on ship
 schedules and CNO availabilities.
 The legacy Navy Cash environment is dependent on a specific hardware and software environment that is costly to maintain and reaching end-of-life on key operating components. Navy Cash 2.0 is the next
 progression in cashless operations that will enhance the current Navy Cash solution and support interoperability with all other DoD service partners (Army, AF, USMC).

 The Navy Cash card is a branded debit card that looks like a typical debit or check card. It utilizes a pre-paid debit model to provide deployed Sailors and Marines 24/7 access to pay and enables a cashless
 shipboard environment for all retail and other personal financial transactions. In addition, Navy Cash cards are an important tool for deployed Sailors and Marines to obtain cash at any worldwide ATM ashore.

 Navy Cash reduces workload for the Disbursing Office and other retail merchants throughout the ship including Ships Store, Food Service, Postal and MWR. This affords more time for core functions and thereby
 increases readiness. It also improves shipboard business practices by automating and significantly reducing the handling of bills, coins, and personal checks, automating back-end financial processes and
 minimizing error-prone cash transactions while it reduces the workload associated with cash management and improves the efficiency and accuracy of fiscal accountability. This overall reduction in the need for
 cash decreases the costs associated with maintaining large amounts of cash in the safes of ships for the six or more months of deployment.

Appropriation / Budget Activity / Budget Sub Activity:                                                       P-1 Line Item Number / Title:
1810N: Other Procurement, Navy / BA 06: Supply Support Equipment / BSA 1:                                    7025 / Supply Equipment
Supply Support Equipment
ID Code (A=Service Ready, B=Not Service Ready): A                       Program Elements for Code B Items: 0708012N                              Other Related Program Elements: 0204996N, 0701112N
Line Item MDAP/MAIS Code: N/A
 NAVAL AUTONOMOUS DATA COLLECTION SYSTEM (NADACS): The Naval Autonomous Data Collection System (NADACS) is an Enterprise level, multi-source, digital tracking tool that supports asset
 visibility, accountability & auditability. The system directly supports both Congressional and Departmental dictates that require Navy to improve its ability to maintain and track material, assets and equipment with
 a high degree of accuracy and accountability.

 NADACS uses a variety of handheld readers, fixed readers, Mesh networking, and sensors to collect asset and combine data from barcoding, Radio Frequency identification (RFID) and Internet of Things (IOT)
 sensors to provide locational and health information of assets which can then be compared with on-hand inventory records.

 NAVSUP will be employing cellular 5G capabilities with Satellite Communication (SATCOM) along with other communication transport methods to move data from point of collection to the NADACS database,
 and then providing end users with a 'Web Browser' picture to assess and manage the asset picture. NADACS is based on government owned software coupled with specialty and commercially available
 hardware to create a complete system. For commercial hardware, the system is hardware agnostic, encouraging use of available hardware while maintaining a high degree of competition.

 Ordnance Information Systems (OIS): Funding supports tech refresh of obsolescent legacy CK71 barcode scanners and printers that require replacement. This improves the warfighter ability to resupply, uses
 less labor and time, and ensures correct shipments.

 [P40A / 1340 20K Diesel Forklift]: NAVSUP FY25 to FY26 Growth Justification:
 Navy is centralizing the procurement of Material Handling Equipment (MHE) to NAVSUP. NAVSUP is scaling to take full ownership of Navy's MHE requirements. As a result, it will cause growth as we address
 MHE deficiencies across the service.

 [P40A / 1350 6K Diesel Forklift]: NAVSUP FY25 to FY26 Growth Justification:
 Navy is centralizing the procurement of Material Handling Equipment (MHE) to NAVSUP. NAVSUP is scaling to take full ownership of Navy's MHE requirements. As a result, it will cause growth as we address
 MHE deficiencies across the service.

 [P40A / 1370 6K Electric Forklift]: NAVSUP FY25 to FY26 Growth Justification:
 Navy is centralizing the procurement of Material Handling Equipment (MHE) to NAVSUP. NAVSUP is scaling to take full ownership of Navy's MHE requirements. As a result, it will cause growth as we address
 MHE deficiencies across the service.

 [P40A / 1396 Aerial Work Platform]: NAVSUP FY25 to FY26 Growth Justification:
 Navy is centralizing the procurement of Material Handling Equipment (MHE) to NAVSUP. NAVSUP is scaling to take full ownership of Navy's MHE requirements. As a result, it will cause growth as we address
 MHE deficiencies across the service.

 [P40A / 5701 Mobile Cleaning, Recovery, & Recycle System (MCRRS)]: NAVSUP FY25 to FY26 Growth Justification:
 Navy is centralizing the procurement of Material Handling Equipment (MHE) to NAVSUP. NAVSUP is scaling to take full ownership of Navy's MHE requirements. As a result, it will cause growth as we address
 MHE deficiencies across the service.

 Justification:
 MHE Control Overview supports the procurement of Shipboard MHE and shore based Commercial Off the Shelf (COTS) MHE.

 Supply Equipment / Shipboard Program and Commercial Off-The-Shelf (COTS) Program
 FY26 increase ($4.49M) supports procurement of Shipboard Material Handling Equipment (MHE) and shore-based COTS MHE. Procurement strategy requires a comprehensive analysis of the 6-year
 established COTS known requirement. Every year, procurements are prioritized by demand signal from the Warfighter and leveraging procurements through bundled contract efficiency.

 Ordnance Information System (OIS) Tech Refresh
 FY26 increase ($0.380M) supports tech refresh/updates required for OIS. Without replacements, a Warfighter will have to manually track ordnance (pen & paper) which will require more labor and increase
 mistakes. The current global environment requires the Warfighter to have the most up-to-date whereabouts of ordnance to be prepared for any situation.

 Description:
 First Destination Transportation (FDT). FDT delivers material from a procurement source to the first point of use or storage which may be in the Continental United States or overseas. The procurement source
 may be a supplier outside of the Department of Defense (DoD) supply system or a DoD activity which fabricates new material. FDT also includes transportation from point of origin to point of use or storage when
 Navy-owned material or equipment is provided to a contractor incident to a Research and Development project or a system/equipment acquisition or modification.

 Justification:
 The FY 2026 request was reduced by $0.003 million for civilian personnel to optimize the workforce in compliance with Executive Order 14210, "Implementing the President's Department of Government
 Efficiency Workforce Optimization Initiative."

 FY 2026 funding required for transportation costs for shipments of OPN-procured equipment and material to the first point of use or storage, or as Government Furnished Equipment/Government Furnished
 Material (GFE/GFM) provided to a contractor incidental to an OPN-funded system/equipment acquisition or modification. The funding increase from FY 2025 to FY 2026 is a result of increased transportation
 costs associated with the trucking industry, as well as a shortage of truck operators.

Initial Spares ($ in Millions)                               -              -               -              -              -               -              -               -         -            -         -              -
Flyaway Unit Cost ($ in Dollars)                             -              -               -              -              -               -              -               -         -            -         -              -
Gross/Weapon System Unit Cost ($ in Dollars)                 -              -               -              -              -               -              -               -         -            -         -              -


 Description:
 Commander, Naval Education and Training Command stated Battle Stations-21 (BST-21) is a mandatory graduation requirement. Additionally, BST-21 is a Fleet requirement for Accessions Level Training (Block
 0) of the Ready Relevant Learning (RRL) initiative.

 BST-21 is a facility that represents a 210-foot-long Arleigh Burke class destroyer. It is the culminating training event for Recruit Training Command recruits to participate in a 12-hour simulation of four (4) General
 Quarters (GQ) scenarios and thirteen (13) shipboard scenarios that include engineering, lookout, and mass casualty drills.

 [P40A / BST-21 AUDIOVISUAL LIGHTING SYSTEMS]: Funding provided for updates to Audiovisual Lighting Systems to train recruits.

 [P40A / BST-21 SUPPORT SYSTEM]: Funding also provides for upgrades to operating equipment, software modifications, and UPS subsystem; ocean fill valve, pit sump pumps Variable Frequency Drive (VFD)
 controllers, blocking valve (natural gas) and power panel, pier stores, and tour brief station.

 [P40A / BST-21 INFORMATION ASSURANCE SOFTWARE MODIFICATION]: Funding provides for BST-21 Information Assurance Software. Software operating system technical refreshes are required every
 three to five years to maintain DOD Cybersecurity Security Controls Certification.

 [P40A / BST-21 SCENIC AND PROPS]: The Scenic and Props requirement provides for the life cycle sustainment plan divided into six separate subcategories: pier front, pier stores, ship exterior, 01 level, main
 deck, and 2nd deck with over 100 hardware items varying in quantities from 1 to 176 to a generic title of "lot".

 [P40A / BST-21 TRAINING MANAGEMENT SYSYTEM (TMS)]: Funds provided support for BST-21 systems tech refresh for training management systems, as well as software modifications.

 [P40A / BST-21 SPECIAL EFFECTS]: Funding decreased due to reduced requirements for special effects.

 [P40A / BST-21 OVERALL SUPERVISORY SYSTEM]: Funding provides refreshes to Overall Supervisory System (OSS) subsystem.

 Justification:

 Description:
 (BSO 60) Training Support Equipment funding provides the Live, Virtual, and Constructive (LVC) training capability necessary to meet critical mission essential requirements for training combat-ready for world-
 wide operations. The LVC capability includes the training support equipment/systems used in support of and connected to the Navy Continuous Training Capability (NCTE) and all of the equipment/systems
 required, including all training systems, network equipment, information technology (IT) equipment, communications equipment, Command, Control, Communications, Computers and Intelligence (C4I) systems,
 and training devices used for LVC training capability.

 This budget line item also provides the resources to maintain the four (4) Training Support Vessels (TSV) in the Atlantic Fleet.

 East Coast geography and range encroachment mandate the Atlantic Fleet use TSVs to provide open ocean Opposing Force (OPFOR) (i.e., live fire, target services, electromagnetic emissions, and jamming
 services) and range services. The Fleet Training Continuum (FTC) instruction directs the Navy to adopt an integrated LVC capability to train Navy forces to high-end threats. Navy procured a fifth (5th) TSV
 (Operational in FY23) to support the Jacksonville Shallow Water Training Range (JSWTR) and is required to sustain four (4) existing TSVs that support Large Scale Integrated Training Events. Naval Sea
 Systems Command (NAVSEA) completed a study and solicited Requests for Information (RFI), then determined the best course of action was to obtain an existing American Bureau of Shipping (ABS) certified
 Offshore Supply Vessel (OSV) for conversion to a TSV configuration. Navy requires all ships to have ABS certification.

 (BSO 22): At the U.S. Naval Academy, the Academic and Professionally Accredited Enterprise Education Enclave (AEEE) system must ensure the best educated and most qualified junior officers enter the naval
 service. Planned upgrades and replacements are vital in ensuring graduates are technologically prepared to serve in tomorrow's Fleet and Fleet Marine Force while supporting institutional accreditation and
 competitiveness with peer institutions.

 NETC recruits, trains, and delivers Sailors to the fleet. SIPR Datacenter, Training Network (TRANET) and associated equipment provides NETC classified/unclassified internet and provides the network
 infrastructure and architecture required for delivery, management, and sustainment of Navy training content across the entire NETC training enterprise. Voice Over Internet Protocol (VOIP) required for increase
 telecom infrastructure modernization across multiple NETC commands.

 [P40A / [USFFC N7] LIFE CYCLE MANAGEMENT (LCM)]: Funding provides for the phased replacement of hardware and software required within the Navy Continuous Training Environment (NCTE). The NCTE
 Enterprise provides sustained adherence to cyber security/Information Assurance (IA) certification and accreditation requirements.

 [P40A / [USFFC N7] BALLISTIC MISSILE DEFENSE (BMD)]: Funding provides for Ballistic Missile Defense (BMD) and Integrated Air Missile Defense (IAMD) mission area Fleet sustainment training and mission
 rehearsal in theater, allow ships to participate in Navy Component Commander mandated BMD/IAMD exercises while pier side or underway, as well as enhance BMD/IAMD training objective accomplishment in
 current Fleet Readiness Training Plan (FRTP) underway training events such as Composite Training Unit Exercise (COMPTUEX) and Joint Training Unit Exercise (JTFEX).

 [P40A / [USFFC N7] BMD FLEET SYNTHETIC TRAINING (FST) AT SEA]: Funding provides for Ballistic Missile Defense (BMD) and Integrated Air Missile Defense (IAMD) mission area Fleet sustainment
 training and mission rehearsal in theater, allow ships to participate in Navy Component Commander mandated BMD/IAMD exercises while pier side or underway, as well as enhance BMD/IAMD training objective
 accomplishment in current Fleet Readiness Training Plan (FRTP) underway training events such as Composite Training Unit Exercise (COMPTUEX) and Joint Training Unit Exercise (JTFEX).

 [P40A / [USFFC N7] ANTI-ACCESS AREA DENIAL (A2AD)]: Funding provides for Gateway portable embarkation kits (PEKs) for use against the high end A2AD, cyber, Information Warfare (IW), and Electronic
 Warfare (EW) threat during Carrier Strike Group (CSG) Navy training events/exercises.

 [P40A / [USFFC N7] NAVAL INTEGRATED FIRE CONTROL - COUNTER AIR (NIFC-CA)]: Funding provides engineering support for Cooperative Engagement Capability (CEC) Interim Training (CIT) System
 portable embarkation kit (PEK) to support Carrier Strike Group (CSG) Fleet training requirements.

 [P40A / [USFFC N7] IMPASS SCORING SYSTEM]: Funding provides for the phased replacement of the Integrated Maritime Portable Acoustic Scoring and Simulation (IMPASS) system, programmed for even
 years.

 [P40A / [USFFC N7] LVC ISO FLEET TRAINING WHOLENESS (FTW)]: Funding provides for procurement of equipment to support Fleet Synthetic Training (FST) at Sea capability for non-Ballistic Missile
 Defense (BMD) Aegis and Ship Self-Defense System (SSDS) platforms and procurement of Total Ship Training Capability (TSTC) shipboard synthetic radios required for realistic Command and Control (C2)
 representation in a Live, Virtual, and Constructive (LVC) environment.

 [P40A / [USFFC N7] EAST COAST NETWORK CONSOLIDATION]: Funding provides technical refresh and sustainment of network consolidation equipment integrating East Coast tactical training ranges and
 systems with the Navy Continuous Training Environment (NCTE).

 [P40A / [USFFC N7] WEST COAST NETWORK CONSOLIDATION]: Funding provides technical refresh and sustainment of network consolidation equipment integrating West Coast tactical training ranges and
 systems with the Navy Continuous Training Environment (NCTE).

 [P40A / [USFFC N7 / CSG4] TRAINING SUPPORT VESSEL (TSV) PROCURE & SUSTAIN]: Funding provides for the availability, utility, and long-term viability of Training Support Vessels (TSVs) support to
 Multi-Ship Group Sail (GRUSL) Exercises, Independent Deployer Certification Exercise (IDCERTEX), Composite Training Unit Exercise (COMPTUEX), and Afloat Training Group (ATG) training events.

 [P40A / [USFFC N7] NCTE CROSS DOMAIN SOLUTION FOR LVC TRAINING]: Funding provides for classification levels of the Enterprise Network Guard (ENG) Raise the Bar (RTB) solution to be sustained.
 NCTE Cross Domain Enterprise Solution (NCDES) is required for unit, integrated and advanced training/certification at all Live, Virtual, and Constructive (LVC) training ranges.

 [P40A / [USFFC N7] WEST COAST LVC NETWORK CONSOLIDATION PHASE 2]: Funding provides for consolidation of Tactical Training Range (TTR) networks into the Navy Continuous Training Environment
 (NCTE) network (i.e., Navy Enterprise Tactical Training Network (NETTN)) to meet Fleet training requirements and network operations and support (communications, systems administrators, and cybersecurity).

 [P40A / [USFFC N7 / CSG4] TRAINING SUPPORT VESSEL (TSV) MAINT BASELINE ADJ]: Funding provides for dry docking intervals and regular intermediate maintenance as required by the American Bureau
 of Shipping (ABS). Dry docking costs have accelerated more than inflation based on several factors: dry dock limitations (fewer interested bidders in a constrained environment), steel pricing schedules and
 Training Support Vessels (TSV) age.

 [P40A / [USFFC N7] STRIKE GROUP MARITIME OPEN OCEAN RANGE (SGMOOVR)]: Funding provides an interim solution (via three (3) Fly Away Kits) for deployed Strike Groups (SG) to conduct Live,
 Virtual, Constructive (LVC) mission rehearsal events in preparation for Combatant Commander's (CCMD) tasking.

 [P40A / [USFFC N7] NCTE CYBER HARDENING]: Funding provides for the procurement and installation of software and hardware solutions on the Navy Continuous Training Environment (NCTE) to align the
 Naval Enterprise Tactical Training network (NETTN) with DoD Zero Trust (ZT) cybersecurity requirements.

 [P40A / [USFFC N7] SURFACE LVC TRAINING]: Funding procures hardware and infrastructure to support increased Live Virtual Constructive (LVC) training event support to Fleet surface units at Fleet
 concentration centers on both East Coast and West Coast.

 [P40A / [USFFC N7] LVC CAPABILITY FOR CVW-5/FDNFJ]: Funding provides for support and equipment necessary to field, integrate, operate, and sustain an Iwakuni CVW-5 "Mini-EXCON" LVC training
 capability for local exercise control of integrated fixed wing aircraft mission trainers and simulators.

 [P40A / FIRE ARMS TRAINING SIMULATOR (FATS)]: Funds provided for procurement of shore-based Firearms Training Simulators (FATS). The simulators provide weapons systems and scenarios for students
 to train while freely maneuvering and handling weapons.

 [P40A / YPYTD - TRAINING DELIVERY SERVICES (TDS)]: Virtual Desktop Infrastructure Initiative (VDI). The training enterprise is moving forward with implementing more efficient technologies to meet the
 training delivery mission via a virtual desktop infrastructure solution. The virtual desktop solution will be implemented over the next several years at each location in lieu of conducting the traditional one-for-one
 workstation refresh. The goals and benefits of VDI are to establish an agile, sustainable enterprise training delivery environment; standardize the processes, services and technology used to deliver training;
 improve the enterprise security posture; refresh outdated electronic classroom workstations and operating systems; and provide a solution that enables a streamlined, centralized IT workforce.

 [P40A / NETC Classroom for Increased Accessions - Instructor Podium Equip]: Funds provided for monitors, instructor podium cables, audio-visual components, SMART Board Interactive Flat Panels,
 Test Kits, and initial warranty.

 [P40A / New NETC Classrooms for Incr. Acc.- Cable Infrastructure Upgrades]: Funding supports NETC implementing a Virtual Desktop Infrastructure (VDI) solution that largely eliminates physical workstations in
 classrooms. Under VDI, students utilize "zero client desktop devices" which provide a user experience similar to physical workstations, without the use of CPU, local storage or Operating Systems.

 [P40A / TRANET Capability Upgrades - Routing/Switching]: Funding supports TRANET Capability Upgrades. The upgrades support CISCO ISR 4431 and ISCO Catalyst 9410 routing and switching equipment.

 [P40A / TRANET Capability Upgrades - Base Area Network Upgrades]: Funds provided for Single Mode Fiber Installation - Catalyst 9300L 24p data, Network Advantage, 4x10G Uplink.

 [P40A / SIPR Data Center Consolidation (DCC)]: NETCs SIPR Datacenter: Provides the hosting for delivery of Sailor 2025/RRL modernized learning content in the classified environment. Supported functions:
 delivery of classified learning content, instructional content development, and advancement exams for classified ratings. SIPR DC provides support for NETCs portfolio of IT Systems, classified (SIPR/NNPI)
 training environment and users. Support includes classified infrastructure, physical environment, information assurance security controls, servers and storage.

 [P40A / SIPR Data Center]: NETC's SIPR Datacenter, provides the hosting for delivery of Sailor 2025/Ready Relevant Learning (RRL) Training modernized learning content in the classified environment.
 Supported functions: delivery of classified learning content, instructional content development, and advancement exams for classified ratings. SIPR DC provides support to NETC'S portfolio of IT Systems,
 classified (SIPR/NNPI) training environment and users. Support includes infrastructure, physical environment, information assurance security controls, servers and storage.

 [P40A / Underwater Equipment]: This funding supports for Cryptologic Technician Maintenance (CTM) and Cryptologic Technician Technical (CTT). Cryptologic Technicians (Maintenance) (CTM) maintain,
 configure, and install intelligence, tactical cryptologic and ancillary systems used to conduct Information and Cyberspace Operations; design, install, test, certify, troubleshoot, and repair or replace: tactical
 cryptologic systems, physical security systems, electrical and electronic equipment, antennas, computer systems, networks, and ancillary equipment; implement Information Assurance (IA) policy; manage
 organizational level maintenance; coordinate repair of command, control, communications, computer, intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance systems in support of surface, subsurface, air, special warfare,
 and national consumers. Cryptologic Technicians (Technical) (CTT) conduct Electronic Warfare (EW) in support of National and Commanders' tasking; operate and maintain electronic sensors and computer
